threats.title: MorphoBlueConnector _getPositionTVL Adds Borrow Amount Instead of Subtracting, Inflating Vault TVL
threats.hypothesis_description: MorphoBlueConnector._getPositionTVL computes TVL as `supplyAmount + borrowAmount + collateralValueInLoan`. The borrowAmount should be SUBTRACTED, not added. Additionally, when borrow() is called, borrowed tokens land in the connector and are tracked as a separate token holding position via _updateTokenInRegistry. This double-counts borrowed value: once positively in the Morpho position TVL and once as held tokens. Total inflation = 2 * borrowValue over the correct net equity.
threats.investigation_rationale: TVL is the foundation of share pricing for deposits and withdrawals. Any inflation directly translates to economic loss for depositors (fewer shares) or excess withdrawal payouts. This is a clear arithmetic error in production code, not a configuration issue.

proof_of_signal.pattern: tvl = _getValue(params.loanToken, base, supplyAmount + borrowAmount + convertCToL(...)). borrowAmount is added positively; should be subtracted for net equity.

preconditions: Vault has a MorphoBlue position with non-zero borrow
Morpho connector is registered as an active connector
Manager calls borrow() to take a loan on Morpho Blue

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Any Morpho borrow inflates vault TVL by 2x the borrow value, affecting all share price calculations
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Manager executes borrow as normal strategy operation; no malicious intent required
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent while any Morpho borrow position exists

operational_questions: What is the actual TVL inflation for typical Morpho borrow positions?
How does the inflated TVL affect deposit share calculations and withdrawal payouts?
Can a manager sequence borrow → calculateWithdrawShares → repay to extract excess value?
validation_requirements: Deploy MorphoBlueConnector with a Morpho market, execute borrow, compare TVL before/after
Verify that borrow tokens appear both in _getPositionTVL and as held token balance
Calculate share price distortion for representative borrow amounts

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Manager borrows maximum allowed amount on Morpho Blue market
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: TVL inflates by 2x the borrow value; share price increases proportionally
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Manager calls recordProfitForFee to capture performance fees on phantom profit
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Or: new deposits receive fewer shares; existing withdrawal calculations overpay
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Only requires normal keeper/manager operations on Morpho Blue

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: No admin compromise needed; code bug triggers during normal operations
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All share pricing is corrupted whenever Morpho borrows exist
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires code fix and potential retroactive rebalancing of affected deposits/withdrawals
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: deposit, withdraw, calculateDepositShares, calculateWithdrawShares, recordProfitForFee, collectPerformanceFees

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic understanding of Morpho Blue borrowing
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time Morpho positions are active

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Monitor Morpho borrow positions and corresponding TVL changes
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: TVL jumps disproportionate to actual value changes after Morpho borrows
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Performance fee extraction correlated with Morpho borrow activity

threats.title: Registry onlyVaultMaintainer Modifier Uses OR Instead of AND, Breaking Core Admin Functions
threats.hypothesis_description: The onlyVaultMaintainer modifier uses `||` (OR) in the revert condition: `if (msg.sender != maintainer || hasRole(EMERGENCY_ROLE, msg.sender) == false)`. Due to De Morgan's law, this requires BOTH conditions to pass (caller must be maintainer AND have EMERGENCY_ROLE). The intended logic should use `&&` (AND) in the revert condition to allow EITHER role. This blocks addConnector, updateConnectorTrustedTokens, and removeTrustedPosition for the vault maintainer unless they also hold the global EMERGENCY_ROLE.
threats.investigation_rationale: Comparison with onlyVaultGoverner and onlyVaultMaintainerWithoutTimeLock confirms they use `&&` (correct). The deployment scripts set all roles to the same address, masking the bug in testing. In production with role separation, these critical admin functions become inaccessible.
threats.config_evidence: deploySharedContracts.ts and updateRegistry.ts show all roles assigned to same EOA (0x8E95f959f1Bd3C4A3Be2bda6089155012fF1a37b), masking this bug in testing

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (msg.sender != vaults[_vaultId].maintainer || hasRole(EMERGENCY_ROLE, msg.sender) == false) — uses || instead of &&. Compare with onlyVaultGoverner at lines 40-43 which correctly uses &&.

preconditions: Vault maintainer address differs from emergency role holder (proper role separation)
Maintainer does NOT hold EMERGENCY_ROLE in global AccessControl

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: In production with role separation, maintainer cannot add connectors or update trusted tokens
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Not an admin abuse — admin is BLOCKED from performing legitimate functions
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent in any deployment with proper role separation

operational_questions: Does the production deployment assign EMERGENCY_ROLE to the maintainer timelock?
Are there any vaults deployed where maintainer != emergency address?
What is the operational impact of being unable to add connectors?
validation_requirements: Test addConnector with maintainer address that does not hold EMERGENCY_ROLE
Verify revert behavior matches the bug analysis
Compare with onlyVaultGoverner modifier to confirm intended pattern

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deploy vault with properly separated roles (maintainer ≠ emergency)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Maintainer attempts to call addConnector — transaction reverts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Vault is unable to add new connectors or update trusted tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Protocol is stuck with initial configuration; new strategies cannot be deployed
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — this is a code defect, not an attack

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Core admin functions completely blocked for non-emergency maintainers
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Vault cannot add new connectors, update trusted tokens, or remove trusted positions
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires governor to reassign maintainer role to an address also holding EMERGENCY_ROLE, breaking role separation

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: None — bug manifests automatically with proper role separation
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: First attempt to call affected functions after deployment

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed transactions to addConnector from maintainer addresses
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: All vault admin operations requiring onlyVaultMaintainer failing

threats.title: AerodromeConnector _getPositionTVL Ignores Gauge-Staked LP Tokens, Understating TVL
threats.hypothesis_description: _getPositionTVL only counts `IERC20(pool).balanceOf(address(this))` for Aerodrome LP positions. However, the stake() function deposits LP tokens into the gauge, removing them from the connector's balance. After staking, the staked LP becomes invisible to TVL calculation, systematically understating vault TVL by the staked amount.
threats.investigation_rationale: Staking LP into gauges is a core operation for earning rewards. If TVL drops when LP is staked, share price drops, enabling new depositors to buy shares cheaply. When LP is later unstaked, TVL jumps back, creating artificial profit/loss cycles.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 balance = IERC20(pool).balanceOf(address(this)) — only counts unstaked LP. Compare with CurveConnector.totalLpBalanceOf which includes gauge balance.

preconditions: Aerodrome LP tokens are staked into the gauge via stake()
TVL calculation is triggered (deposit/withdraw share calculations)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: After manager calls stake(), TVL drops by the full staked LP value
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Normal strategy operation triggers the bug

operational_questions: What percentage of Aerodrome positions are typically staked?
How does the TVL drop affect share pricing for concurrent deposits/withdrawals?
Can the timing of stake/unstake be exploited by informed depositors?
validation_requirements: Stake Aerodrome LP and verify TVL before/after
Compare _getPositionTVL with actual value including gauge balance

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Observe that Aerodrome LP is staked in gauge (TVL drops)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Wait for LP to be unstaked (TVL recovers)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Withdraw at higher share price, extracting value from existing holders
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires observing on-chain state and timing deposits around stake/unstake events

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: None needed — code bug in normal operations
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Share price systematically understated when Aerodrome LP is staked
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires code fix to include gauge balance in TVL
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All TVL-dependent operations: deposit share calculation, withdrawal calculation, fee recording

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Share price drops without actual loss of value

threats.title: resetMiddle Does Not Reset totalCBAmount, Causing Double-Counting of Withdrawal Amounts
threats.hypothesis_description: When resetMiddle moves the withdraw queue middle pointer backwards, calculateWithdrawShares re-processes previously calculated withdrawal requests. The amounts are additively accumulated into currentWithdrawGroup.totalCBAmount without clearing the prior calculations. This double-counts withdrawal amounts, inflating totalCBAmount. The inflated amount flows into neededAssetsForWithdraw and fulfillCurrentWithdrawGroup, causing excess token retrieval from connectors and incorrect pro-rata distribution in executeWithdraw.
threats.investigation_rationale: resetMiddle is designed for price manipulation recovery, but the lack of totalCBAmount reset creates a silent accounting corruption. The manager calling resetMiddle followed by recalculation would not know totalCBAmount is double-counted.

proof_of_signal.pattern: resetMiddle sets withdrawQueue.middle = newMiddle but does not reset currentWithdrawGroup.totalCBAmount. calculateWithdrawShares at line 194 does currentWithdrawGroup.totalCBAmount += assetsNeededForWithdraw, re-adding previously counted amounts.

preconditions: Manager calls resetMiddle for withdraw queue (moving middle backwards)
Manager subsequently calls calculateWithdrawShares to re-process
currentWithdrawGroup not yet started (isStarted == false)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: resetMiddle → calculateWithdrawShares → totalCBAmount has double-counted entries
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Manager triggers this through legitimate error recovery workflow

operational_questions: What is the magnitude of double-counting relative to withdrawal amounts?
Does the inflated totalCBAmount cause neededAssetsForWithdraw to over-request from connectors?
How does the pro-rata distribution (totalABAmount/totalCBAmountFullfilled) distort payouts?
validation_requirements: Simulate resetMiddle followed by recalculation and verify totalCBAmount
Trace through fulfillCurrentWithdrawGroup with inflated totalCBAmount
Verify executeWithdraw payout calculations with double-counted amounts

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Withdrawal requests queued and calculated (totalCBAmount = X)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Manager calls resetMiddle to move middle backwards
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Manager calls calculateWithdrawShares again (totalCBAmount = X + Y, where Y re-counts overlapping items)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Withdrawal group proceeds with inflated totalCBAmount causing excess retrieval or incorrect distribution

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Accounting corruption through legitimate admin workflow
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Withdrawal amounts incorrectly calculated; pro-rata distribution skewed
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires manual correction of totalCBAmount or code fix
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All withdrawal operations in the affected group

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low — two manager calls in sequence

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: totalCBAmount exceeding sum of individual withdrawal amounts

threats.title: Withdraw Group State Machine Allows Orphaned Calculations During Fulfilled-But-Not-Executed State
threats.hypothesis_description: calculateWithdrawShares only blocks when `isFullfilled == false && isStarted == true`. When the group is fulfilled (isStarted=true, isFullfilled=true), calculateWithdrawShares proceeds and accumulates new amounts into totalCBAmount. When executeWithdraw finishes and deletes currentWithdrawGroup, these accumulated amounts are lost. The next withdrawal group starts with totalCBAmount = 0 instead of including the orphaned calculations.
threats.investigation_rationale: The state machine guard in calculateWithdrawShares at line 181 checks `currentWithdrawGroup.isFullfilled == false && currentWithdrawGroup.isStarted == true` for revert. The (true, true) state passes this check, allowing calculation during an unsafe window.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Guard at line 181: if (isFullfilled == false && isStarted == true) revert. When both are true, guard passes. Line 194: totalCBAmount += assetsNeededForWithdraw. Line 228: if (lastId == firstTemp) delete currentWithdrawGroup — destroys accumulated totalCBAmount.

preconditions: Withdraw group is fulfilled but not fully executed (isStarted=true, isFullfilled=true)
New withdrawal requests exist in the queue past the current group's lastId
Manager calls calculateWithdrawShares before executeWithdraw completes

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: fulfillCurrentWithdrawGroup → calculateWithdrawShares → executeWithdraw (delete) → orphaned calculations lost
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Manager operation ordering during normal workflow
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Between fulfillCurrentWithdrawGroup and final executeWithdraw iteration

operational_questions: How likely is this ordering in practice?
What happens to users whose withdrawals were calculated during the orphaned window?
Are their withdrawal amounts permanently lost or can they be recalculated?
validation_requirements: Execute the exact sequence: fulfill → calculate new withdrawals → complete execute
Verify totalCBAmount is destroyed when executeWithdraw finishes
Check if affected withdrawals can be processed in next group

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Withdraw group is fulfilled and partially executed
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Manager calls calculateWithdrawShares for new pending requests
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Manager completes executeWithdraw for current group — delete destroys orphaned totalCBAmount
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Next withdraw group starts with totalCBAmount = 0, orphaned calculations have no effect on new group
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Normal manager operations during high-activity periods

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Withdrawal accounting corrupted through normal operations
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Orphaned withdrawal calculations may cause incorrect token retrieval amounts

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: calculateWithdrawShares events during fulfilled group state

threats.title: FlashLoan Address Has Universal Manager Access Across All Vaults and Connectors
threats.hypothesis_description: The onlyManager modifier in NoyaGovernanceBase grants access to `registry.flashLoan()` — a single global address. This address can call ALL onlyManager functions across every vault and connector, including executeDeposit, executeWithdraw, calculateDepositShares, collectPerformanceFees, swapHoldings, and all connector strategy functions. If this is set to a generic flash loan provider (e.g., Balancer Vault), any user executing a flash loan callback could act as manager.
threats.investigation_rationale: The deployment script sets flashLoan to address(0) initially, but setFlashLoanAddress can be called by MAINTAINER_ROLE to set it to any address. If set to a contract that allows arbitrary callbacks (standard flash loan pattern), the callback caller becomes the effective manager.
deployment_assumptions: flashLoan address is set to a contract with user-triggerable callbacks
threats.config_evidence: deploySharedContracts.ts line: Registry.deploy(ownerAddress, ownerAddress, ownerAddress, '0x0000000000000000000000000000000000000000') — flashLoan initially address(0). Registry.setFlashLoanAddress can change it.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (!(msg.sender == keeperContract || msg.sender == emergencyManager || msg.sender == registry.flashLoan())) revert — flashLoan has equal privilege to keeper and emergency across ALL vaults

preconditions: flashLoan address set to a contract allowing user-triggered callbacks
The callback contract passes msg.sender as the flashLoan address when calling vault/connector functions

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Flash loan callback can call any onlyManager function on any vault
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires maintainer to set flashLoan to a vulnerable address
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent once flashLoan address is set to a callback-enabling contract

operational_questions: What flash loan provider will be used in production?
Does the flash loan contract allow arbitrary callbacks where msg.sender == flashLoan address?
Is there any per-vault scoping of flash loan access?
validation_requirements: Check if production flashLoan address allows user-initiated callbacks
Test calling onlyManager functions from flash loan callback
Verify global scope — single flashLoan address controls all vaults

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify the flashLoan contract and its callback mechanism
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Initiate flash loan; in callback, call executeWithdraw or swapHoldings
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Execute arbitrary manager operations across any vault
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repay flash loan; retain extracted value
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Single atomic transaction via flash loan

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Full manager-level control over all vaults
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Can manipulate deposits, withdrawals, fees, and positions across all vaults
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires emergency pause and flashLoan address reset
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All onlyManager functions across all vaults and connectors

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Medium — requires understanding flash loan callback mechanics
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time after flashLoan address is set

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Manager operations originating from flashLoan address
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpected manager calls not from keeper or emergency
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Flash loan transactions with embedded manager calls

threats.title: UNIv3Connector _getPositionTVL Uses Aggregate Pool Position Key Instead of NFT-Specific Position
threats.hypothesis_description: UNIv3Connector._getPositionTVL computes the position key as `keccak256(abi.encodePacked(positionManager, tL, tU))` and queries `pool.positions(key)`. This returns the AGGREGATE liquidity for ALL NFT positions managed by the NonfungiblePositionManager at the same tick range, not just the connector's specific tokenId. If other users have positions through the same positionManager at identical tick ranges, the connector over-reports TVL.
threats.investigation_rationale: Uniswap V3's NonfungiblePositionManager is a shared contract. The pool tracks positions by (owner, tickLower, tickUpper) where owner is the positionManager contract. Multiple NFTs at the same ticks share this aggregate position entry.

proof_of_signal.pattern: bytes32 key = keccak256(abi.encodePacked(positionManager, tL, tU)); pool.positions(key) — returns aggregate for ALL NFTs at this tick range through positionManager, not just the connector's tokenId.

preconditions: Other users have Uniswap V3 positions through the same NonfungiblePositionManager
Their positions share the exact same tickLower and tickUpper values
Common for popular tick ranges (e.g., full range, standard price ranges)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: TVL reports higher liquidity than the connector actually owns
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — depends on external user behavior
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: When other positions exist at same tick range

operational_questions: How common are identical tick ranges across different NFT positions?
What is the typical magnitude of TVL overstatement?
Should the position query use the NFT's specific liquidity instead?
validation_requirements: Open a position at a popular tick range and compare pool.positions() vs positionManager.positions(tokenId)
Measure the overstatement when other positions exist at same ticks

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify connector's Uniswap V3 position tick range
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Create positions at same tick range (or observe existing positions)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Connector reports inflated TVL including others' liquidity
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Exploit inflated TVL via deposit/withdrawal timing
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Ability to create Uniswap V3 positions at specific tick ranges

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Share price inflated, affecting all depositors and withdrawers
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires code fix to query individual NFT positions

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Medium — requires capital for Uniswap positions
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Understanding of Uniswap V3 position mechanics
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: When connector has active Uniswap V3 positions

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Discrepancy between connector's NFT liquidity and reported TVL
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: TVL higher than expected given connector's actual positions
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: New positions opened at same tick ranges as connector

threats.title: ChainlinkOracleConnector Missing Feed Decimals Normalization Produces Incorrect Token Valuations
threats.hypothesis_description: ChainlinkOracleConnector.getValue uses `getTokenDecimals(decimalsSource)` as the sourceTokenUnit, where decimalsSource is the asset's decimals (non-inverse) or baseToken's decimals (inverse). However, the Chainlink price has its own decimals (typically 8 for USD feeds, 18 for ETH feeds). The formula `amountIn * price / sourceTokenUnit` does not account for the feed's decimal precision. For mismatched token/feed decimals (e.g., 18-decimal token with 8-decimal feed priced into 6-decimal base), the result is off by orders of magnitude.
threats.investigation_rationale: For WETH (18 dec) to USDC (6 dec) with ETH/USD Chainlink (8 dec feed): result = 1e18 * 3000e8 / 1e18 = 3000e8. Expected: 3000e6. Result is 100x too high. This affects ALL TVL calculations using Chainlink pricing.
deployment_assumptions: Chainlink feeds with non-matching decimals relative to token pair decimals
threats.config_evidence: ChainlinkOracleConnector.sol does not call source.decimals() for normalization. Feed decimals are never queried.

proof_of_signal.pattern: getTokenDecimals(decimalsSource) uses token decimals but ignores AggregatorV3Interface.decimals(). Formula: amountIn * price / sourceTokenUnit — price precision is feed-dependent and not normalized.

preconditions: Chainlink feed decimals differ from the expected precision
Token decimals mismatch creates non-canceling precision error
Price source is configured for the token pair

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: getValue returns amounts in incorrect precision depending on feed/token decimal combinations
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: All operations using affected price pairs

operational_questions: What Chainlink feeds are configured in production?
For each feed, what are the token decimals vs feed decimals?
What is the magnitude of the precision error for each configured pair?
validation_requirements: For each configured price pair, compute expected vs actual getValue output
Verify using concrete token pairs (WETH/USDC, WBTC/USDC, etc.)
Check if errors cancel out or compound in multi-hop price routes

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify token pairs with non-canceling decimal mismatches
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: TVL is systematically inflated/deflated due to incorrect valuations
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Deposit when TVL is deflated (get more shares) or withdraw when inflated (get more base tokens)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Profit from the persistent pricing error
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Identifying the specific decimal mismatch and its direction

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Systematic mis-pricing of assets across the vault
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires code fix to normalize feed decimals
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All TVL calculations, share pricing, fee recording

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Persistent whenever affected pairs are used

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: TVL amounts that seem off by powers of 10
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Share prices that don't match expected asset values

threats.title: UniswapValueOracle Silently Truncates Amount to uint128, Producing Incorrect Valuations for Large Balances
threats.hypothesis_description: UniswapValueOracle.getValue casts `uint128 amountIn128 = uint128(amount)`. For amounts exceeding uint128.max (~3.4e38), the cast silently truncates, returning a drastically undervalued result. While rare for mainstream tokens, 18-decimal tokens with extremely large balances or tokens with unusual decimal counts could hit this limit, causing TVL underreporting.
threats.investigation_rationale: Solidity 0.8.x checked arithmetic does NOT catch downcasting truncation. The uint128 cast is unchecked even in safe math mode.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint128 amountIn128 = uint128(amount) — silent truncation for amount > type(uint128).max. No overflow check or revert.

preconditions: Token amount exceeds uint128.max (approximately 3.4e38)
UniswapValueOracle is configured as the price source for the token pair

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Large token balances valued at truncated (near-zero) amounts
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — triggered by token balance size
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: When any token balance exceeds uint128.max

operational_questions: Are there any tokens with supplies/balances that could exceed uint128.max?
What is the practical probability of hitting this limit?
Does this affect any currently deployed token configurations?
validation_requirements: Test getValue with amounts at and above uint128.max
Verify truncation behavior and quantify the error

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify token with extremely large balance (or create via rebasing/low-value tokens)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Oracle returns truncated value, TVL is severely understated
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Deposit at artificially low share price
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Trigger correct TVL recalculation and withdraw at corrected price
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires token with extremely large amounts

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires code fix to handle large amounts or revert
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: TVL calculation, share pricing for affected tokens

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Unexpectedly low TVL for high-balance token positions
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Oracle returning near-zero for large amounts

threats.title: collectManagementFees Can Underflow When Unminted Performance Fee Shares Exceed Total Supply
threats.hypothesis_description: collectManagementFees computes `currentFeeShares = balanceOf(managementFeeReceiver) + balanceOf(performanceFeeReceiver) + preformanceFeeSharesWaitingForDistribution`. The management fee is then calculated on `(totalShares - currentFeeShares)`. Since preformanceFeeSharesWaitingForDistribution represents unminted shares, if this value is large enough, `currentFeeShares > totalShares`, causing an arithmetic underflow revert in Solidity 0.8.x.
threats.investigation_rationale: recordProfitForFee can set preformanceFeeSharesWaitingForDistribution to an arbitrarily large value (bounded by profit * performanceFee). If this exceeds the difference between totalSupply and existing fee receiver balances, management fee collection is permanently blocked.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Line 296: currentFeeShares includes preformanceFeeSharesWaitingForDistribution (not yet minted). Line 299: managementFeeAmount = ... * (totalShares - currentFeeShares) — underflows if currentFeeShares > totalShares.

preconditions: Large profit recorded via recordProfitForFee
preformanceFeeSharesWaitingForDistribution exceeds (totalSupply - existing fee receiver balances)
collectManagementFees called before collectPerformanceFees

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: recordProfitForFee with large profit → collectManagementFees reverts

operational_questions: What profit levels would cause preformanceFeeSharesWaitingForDistribution to exceed totalSupply gap?
Is this scenario likely in normal market conditions?
Does the 12-48 hour performance fee window create a long blocking period?
validation_requirements: Simulate with large profit to trigger the underflow
Verify collectManagementFees reverts
Determine if the protocol can recover after performance fees are collected

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Inflate vault profit (e.g., via Morpho TVL bug or legitimate high returns)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: collectManagementFees reverts due to underflow for 12+ hours
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Management fee collection is DoS'd until performance fees are collected
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None for DoS; requires high profit scenario

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Management fee collection blocked for 12+ hours per occurrence
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Resolves after collectPerformanceFees is called (minting the shares)

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Management fees not collected for extended periods

threats.title: Watchers.verifyRemoveLiquidity Is Empty, Providing No Withdrawal Verification
threats.hypothesis_description: Watchers.verifyRemoveLiquidity is an empty function that performs no validation. BaseConnector.sendTokensToTrustedAddress relies on this function to verify withdrawal amounts when the accounting manager requests tokens. Without verification, a compromised or malicious keeper can extract arbitrary amounts from connectors without any watcher-level oversight.
threats.investigation_rationale: The Watchers contract is documented as a security monitoring role to undo suspicious actions. The empty verification function completely negates this security layer for token withdrawals from connectors to the accounting manager.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function verifyRemoveLiquidity(uint256 withdrawAmount, uint256 sentAmount, bytes memory data) external view { } — completely empty function body, no validation whatsoever

preconditions: Keeper/manager is compromised or acts maliciously
AccountingManager calls connector's sendTokensToTrustedAddress

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Arbitrary token amounts can be extracted from connectors with no watcher validation
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires compromised keeper to exploit; designed security layer is missing

operational_questions: Was this intentionally left as a placeholder for future implementation?
Are there other mechanisms compensating for this missing verification?
Is the Watcher multisig threshold sufficient to prevent single-signer abuse?
validation_requirements: Verify that verifyRemoveLiquidity never reverts regardless of inputs
Test extraction of amounts exceeding legitimate withdrawal needs

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Compromise keeper keys (or collude with keeper signers)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Connector's sendTokensToTrustedAddress calls verifyRemoveLiquidity — passes trivially
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Tokens extracted to accounting manager, then rescued via emergency or routed elsewhere

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Depends on detection speed; assets may already be moved
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All token withdrawals from connectors to accounting manager

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Unusually large token transfers from connectors
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: retrieveTokensForWithdraw amounts exceeding withdrawal group needs
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Token extraction without corresponding withdrawal execution

threats.title: SwapAndBridgeHandler Has No Upper Bound on Slippage Tolerance, Allowing Value Extraction via Malicious Swaps
threats.hypothesis_description: setGeneralSlippageTolerance and setSlippageTolerance have no upper bound validation. The maintainer or emergency role can set slippage to 1e6 (100%) or even higher, effectively disabling slippage protection. With maximum slippage, any swap through the handler would accept 0 output tokens, allowing the swap route to extract the full input amount as MEV/profit.
threats.investigation_rationale: While genericSlippageTolerance defaults to 50,000 (5%), there's no cap enforced. Combined with a malicious or compromised swap route, this creates a value extraction vector through swapHoldings calls.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function setGeneralSlippageTolerance(uint256 _slippageTolerance) external onlyMaintainerOrEmergency { genericSlippageTolerance = _slippageTolerance; } — no upper bound check. If set to 1e6, minAmount becomes 0.

preconditions: Maintainer or emergency role sets slippage tolerance to extreme value
Swaps are executed through the handler with checkForSlippage = true

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: setGeneralSlippageTolerance(1000000) → all swaps accept 0 minimum output
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires maintainer/emergency role to set extreme tolerance
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After tolerance is set until corrected

operational_questions: What is the maximum reasonable slippage tolerance for production operations?
Can pair-specific slippage override the general tolerance to mitigate?
Are there monitoring systems for slippage tolerance changes?
validation_requirements: Set slippage to 100% and execute a swap
Verify that 0 output tokens are accepted
Quantify value extractable through a single swap

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: minAmount calculates to 0 — swap route returns minimal tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Value difference captured by swap route or external MEV

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All swaps execute with no slippage protection

investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Slippage changes followed by large swaps
